question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Committee in Common for Health and Social Care providers.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 32400/22-27, for an update on when the Active Travel Delivery Plan will be published.
answer_text: As stated in my response to AQW 32400/22-27, the public consultation on the draft Active Travel Delivery Plan closed on 28 February 2025. Officials provided written evidence to the Committee on 5 November 2025, followed by an oral evidence session on 18 February 2026. The Committee’s feedback, received on 3 March 2026, is currently being considered as officials finalise the Plan for my consideration.Given the importance of the Plan in shaping future investment and delivery of active travel, I will require time to give careful consideration to the final proposals. I hope to be in a position to publish the Plan in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the delivery of the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan.
answer_text: Of the 37 actions within the Plan, 20 have been delivered and 17 are actively progressing. I remain committed to driving forward the delivery of the remaining actions at pace.  Progress to date reflects coordinated efforts across key themes including scientific research and innovation, enhanced monitoring and enforcement, education and stakeholder engagement, regulatory reform, and strategic communications. Whilst important strides have been made, it is essential to recognise that the recovery of Lough Neagh will require sustained, long-term action.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: We use our engagement with international partners to promote this region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s priorities.Global outreach and inward investment are important factors in helping us to achieve these goals.A new framework for International Relations is under development.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department’s (i) policy; and (ii) minimum standards, for the required square footage, or meterage, of outdoor play space for (a) nursery pupils; and (b) primary school pupils.
answer_text: The Department’s Building Handbook sets out the recommended accommodation provision and outdoor play space for all schools, including nursery schools and primary schools, based on enrolment numbers. The site areas provided in the Handbook are the preferred minimum. However, it is recognised that attaining the minimum is not always possible, particularly within the confines of an urban setting or when redeveloping an existing site. In such circumstances, the Department will give due consideration to the minimum acceptable area on a case-by-case basis.The Building Handbook can be found at https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/articles/building-handbook

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he knew about the impact of the draft NI Reval2026, on hospitality businesses.
answer_text: I was first advised of the emerging valuation impacts across all business sectors in late 2025 and continued to monitor the position through further briefings ahead of publication of the draft list on 22 January 2026. As I set out in my statement to the Assembly on 2 February 2026, I have listened carefully to the concerns raised by the hospitality sector and decided to stop Reval2026 so that we could take stock and allow sectors the space to provide the evidence needed for a full examination of the issues.My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the A1 Junctions Phase 2 project.
answer_text: The A1 Junctions Phase 2 Project will deliver enhanced safety measures along a 25km stretch of the A1 route between Loughbrickland and Hillsborough, costing in the region of £120 - £130m and will include the closing up of all gaps in the central median and the provision of a number of new grade separated junctions.The scheme was confirmed by my predecessor as a single package of works on 2 July 2024 and a restricted list was notified to tenderers on 9 May 2025. I recently instructed officials to extend the restricted list for a period of 12 months to 5 February 2027, continuing to keep this important scheme viable pending the outcome of the A5 appeal. We have to await the Court of Appeal's ruling, because it is important that we consider it fully when looking at the next steps for all major roads projects, as we do not want to be in a scenario where we face challenges on everything.	Whilst I want to see the project delivered at the earliest possible stage, it is critical that we consider the outcome of the appeal before taking the next steps.	Every death on our roads is a profound loss to the community and unfortunately the A1 is a road that has been impacted exponentially by tragic circumstances.  I remain committed to the construction of this vitally important infrastructure safety upgrade, and will take all actions available to me to secure its delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the policy goals that she has been unable to progress through the Executive since the Assembly was restored in 2024.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline any assessment he has made on the long-term impact on the sustainability of community pharmacy services of maintaining the clawback mechanism as a means of managing wider financial pressures across the health system.
answer_text: My Department has invested to support community pharmacy in recent years, with funding increasing by almost £60m (57%) since April 2018. This is the highest level of funding to date and is comparable with the rest of the UK. I recently met with Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) who raised concerns about medicines reimbursement and funding arrangements. CPNI’s request is to adjust or remove established discount clawback arrangements to allow over £20m to be retained by community pharmacies with no additional service provision or benefit for patients or the HSC. This is unrealistic when my Department is experiencing a significant funding gap to meet existing services.  Discount clawback is a mechanism designed not to manage HSC financial pressures but to modulate margin or retained purchase profit as part of medicines reimbursement arrangements. The medicines margin system and annual margin survey ensures that, on average, community pharmacy reimbursement arrangements cover the cost of purchasing medicines plus a level of margin meaning that HSC dispensing should be a profitable activity overall. This system incentivises efficient procurement by pharmacies, yielding value for money for the HSC, while ensuring pharmacies are rewarded for this service. In 2025/26 the guaranteed level of margin for community pharmacy is £34m, increased from £31.5m in 2024/25. In recent years, the actual margin achieved by pharmacies has significantly exceeded the guaranteed level. Unlike other UK regions, any excess margin is not recouped, providing additional income for pharmacies above the agreed financial envelope. My Department’s most recent review of access and delivery of HSC pharmacy services found that Northern Ireland is well provided in respect of access to community pharmacy services and that services are being delivered in line with an agreed commissioning plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any financial support he will put in place for businesses whose premises were flooded due to Storm Chandra.
answer_text: In terms of support from my Department, prior support through the rating system has been made available for flooding in previous occurrences and an assessment can be conducted in relation to the need for similar circumstances by my Department should this be required.Any wider package of support is a matter for the Executive to agree. Following that process previously, flood support was administered by the Department for Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether an economic impact assessment was carried out as part of Reval2026.
answer_text: Revaluation is a statutory exercise carried out under the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977, which sets out the legal framework within which all nondomestic properties must be assessed for rating purposes. The legislation sets the basis of value as a rental value at a fixed valuation date, and the Valuation Service is required to determine Net Annual Values on that basis.The purpose of a revaluation is to ensure that the rates burden is redistributed based on the most up to date market evidence available. This ensures there is fairness in the rating system based on the property market at a point in time. In regard to an economic impact assessment, the revaluation process as the revaluation itself reflects the economy. As the Member will be aware, I announced on 29 January that I was stopping the Reval 2026 process.I would direct the Member to my oral statement to the Assembly:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any training courses available for classroom assistants; (ii) the number of places available on the training courses; and (iii) whether participants receive remuneration for accredited training courses.
answer_text: The Education Authority has provided details of 68 training courses that are currently available to Classroom Assistants. I have deposited this information in the Assembly Library.In addition to the Education Authority courses, as part of the delivery of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Early Childhood intervention programme, 120 SEN-specific training places have been offered to assistants in pre-school and Foundation stage in 2025/26, with a further 240 places planned over the next 2 years.Five Further Education colleges are engaged in delivering a range of accredited courses this academic year which are:OCN NI Level 3 Award in Working with Children and Young People with Special Needs;NCFE CACHE Level 3 Award in Supporting Children and Young People’s Speech, Language and Communication; andOCN NI Level 2 Award in Learning Disability and Positive Support in Early Years.Participants will receive a payment for attendance at and completion of the course, which is outside of contracted hours.In October 2025, I also announced a new partnership with the Chartered College of Teaching, enabling both teachers and classroom assistants to access evidence-informed professional learning programmes. This partnership supports the TransformED Strategy commitment to helping education staff access and use research effectively and represents an important step in delivering on that commitment.For 2025/26, funding has been made available for 100 classroom assistants to receive associate membership of the Chartered College and access to a range of tailored professional learning courses. This programme recognises the valuable contribution made by classroom assistants and supports their ongoing professional development as integral members of the school community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why the Department's Track a Fault online reporting portal has updated reference numbers with, defects superseded by new enquiries, across the Southern Division.

A unique reference number is provided and can be used to access updates to the report. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 36825/22-27, to detail (i) the rationale as to why the Driver and Vehicle Agency limits candidates to three attempts at this test, whereas Great Britain permits unlimited attempts within a two-year period; and (ii) whether consideration will be given to aligning Northern Ireland with Great Britain.
answer_text: As you will be aware, driver licensing is a transferred matter (NI Act 1998 [1998 c47 part 1 Section 4] and as such driver licensing policy differs between here and Britain.The Motor Cars (Driving Instruction) Regulations (NI) 2004 consolidated with amendments the earlier Motor Cars (Driving Instruction) Regulations (NI) 1991. Regulation 9(3) of the 2004 Regulations introduced the restriction regarding number of attempts at the ADI theory test:“Where a person has failed the theory test on his third attempt he shall not be eligible to take it again for a period of two years commencing on the date of the first attempt.”The restrictions were introduced to maintain high professional standards, improve road safety and encourage preparedness in candidates. This was following a public consultation in 2002 on the review of the ADI scheme here, wherein 91% of respondents expressed support for a three-attempt limit on the theory test.Road safety continues to be of paramount importance for my Department, and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. There are no current plans to change the three-limit attempt for ADI theory test.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has given any consideration to rate relief for single occupant households, as part of the Rating Policy: Strategic Roadmap.
answer_text: A single occupancy discount was given detailed consideration by the then Executive during prior changes to the domestic rating system. It was ruled out at that time, with such a widespread discount seen as difficult to justify on grounds of cost, need and vulnerability to fraud. That latter point is critical. In England alone, where a Council Tax system with a personal tax element is factored into the tax structure, for the period between 2020 to 2022 a total of 32,669 incorrect claims were detected for the Council Tax Single Person Discount, leading to £44.7m fraudulent or wrongly received payments. This was reported on within the 2022 National Fraud Initiative Report.As regards the Strategic Review of Rates Supports currently being undertaken, an up to date assessment can be undertaken on this position in the context of the review of the Lone Pensioner Allowance within our rating system. That more controlled and targeted measure saw over £10m of support provided locally to 39,000 pensioners aged 70 or over and living alone receive a 20% reduction in their rates liability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any progress on the three-year strategic plan for health and social care published in December 2024, including the outcome to expand the bowel screening programme, subject to additional funding, by April 2027.
answer_text: The three-year strategic plan, published in December 2024, sets a clear direction for the reform of Northern Ireland’s Health & Social Care System over a three-year period to March 2027. This plan should be read in conjunction with the HSC Reset Plan, published in July 2025, which commits to reconfiguring and resetting our system to ensure we can continue to deliver high quality health & social care in the most efficient, effective & sustainable way possible.Notable progress has been made on the delivery of the Three-Year plan, including:In Elective Care, since March 2025, 164,000 red flag and time critical patients have been seen, diagnosed or treated; the number of patients waiting over four years for an outpatient assessment reduced by 35% and the number of patients waiting for an inpatient or day case procedure reduced by 47%. Furthermore, 70,000 Elective Care interventions are on track to be delivered by March 2026 in line with the Programme for Government commitment.A further £61m investment is planned into the Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDT) programme to expand the range of services available in primary care over the next 4 years and help address the high levels of demand in GP Practices. The implementation of the MDT model across 126 GP practices will increase access for 845,398 (42% of the NI Population) patients to one or more core MDT services across NI. The plan also commits to tackling health inequalities. Under the ‘Making Life Better’ Programme, two pilots in Belfast and Londonderry have concluded. Evaluation suggests that 78% of those who attended a health check reported an increased awareness of how to improve their health, 80% reported they were more likely to make changes to improve their health, 75% felt motivated to access the health care they need and 75% had increased awareness of health services in their local area. A thematic report considering all the findings and the way forward is due to be published in early 2026, the results of which will feed into the ongoing development of the neighbourhood model of care.In terms of the expansion of the Bowel Screening Programme specifically, work is progressing to bring our arrangements in line with UK National Screening Committee’s recommendation, and the ambition laid out in the NI Cancer Strategy (2022-32). The Public Health Agency has commenced a project with the aim of producing a business case and implementation plan for the expansion of the Bowel Screening Programme, reducing age eligibility from aged 60 currently to aged 50.  The business case is expected to be submitted this month (December 2025).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether he has engaged with the UK Government regarding their formal response to the The Law Commission of England and Wales' 2019 report entitled A New Weddings Law.
answer_text: The marriage law in England and Wales differs markedly from our own in a number of respects and, as a result, recommendations as to how it might be amended are not of particular relevance here. Many of the reforms proposed by the Law Commission of England and Wales, including a more straightforward notification process, recognition of belief marriage and a greater choice of wedding venue, are already in place in this jurisdiction and have been for many years. I have not engaged with the Westminster Government with regard to its response to the report of the Law Commission of England and Wales on the marriage law in that jurisdiction. My Department will, however, keep in view the legislative process the Westminster Government plans to undertake in the new year following its response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQO 525/22-27, for an update on whether he has given consideration to including independent celebrants in the upcoming Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill.
answer_text: Legislating to enable independent celebrants to perform legally binding marriages was carefully considered at all stages during the development of the Bill, including public consultation. Consultation responses on independent celebrants were mixed and inconclusive, recognising independent celebrants would require fundamental and changes to the existing and settled law, notably the current rules regarding the provision of marriage for profit and gain. Executive agreement to proceed with the policy and the drafting of a Bill was secured without reference to changing the law relating to independent celebrants. I am keen to progress the Bill and the important reforms it contains as soon as possible, particularly those relating to the increase in minimum age. My Department will keep issues such as independent celebrants in view.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has given consideration to reducing the age eligibility for the bowel cancer screening programme, in line with the UK National Screening Committee recommendations.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy (2022-32) has committed to reducing the sensitivity levels used in the Faecal Immunochemical Test (FIT) tests and to lowering the age range in the NI Bowel Cancer Screening Programme, in line the UK National Screening Committee recommendation. The current UK National Screening Committee recommendation for bowel cancer screening is that it should be offered every 2 years to men and women between the ages of 50 and 74 years old using the FIT test at a sensitivity threshold of 20 µg Hb/g.  Currently in Northern Ireland, men and women aged between 60 and 74 years old, who are registered with a GP, are eligible for bowel cancer screening. Work to meet the commitments to expand the bowel screening programme is ongoing but must be viewed within the context of wider financial and capacity challenges within the supporting services. Where a Screening Programme expands its eligibility, it is important that the associated infrastructure, including diagnostic capacity, workforce and any treatments that may be required, are aligned.I remain committed to ensuring that the NI Bowel Screening Programme is expanded in line with UK National Screening Committee recommendations and as outlined in my three-year HSC Reset Plan, subject to future departmental spending priorities.Regardless of age, anyone experiencing any symptoms of concern should contact their GP immediately.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given any consideration to removing the two-year restriction on reapplying for the Approved Driving Instructor qualification in Northern Ireland after three failed attempts, given that no such restriction exists in Great Britain.
answer_text: To become an Approved Driving Instructor (ADI) both here and in Britain, an applicant must carry out three qualifying tests. Part 1 tests their theory knowledge, Part 2 tests their driving ability and fitness to drive and Part 3 tests their ability to instruct learner drivers.In both jurisdictions where a candidate has failed one or more parts of the qualification process there is a two-year restriction on them re-applying to become an ADI. However, how this restriction is applied differs slightly between here and Britain. The Part 1 (theory test) element of the ADI qualification is the only element of our process that differs from that in Britain and it also impacts when they can restart the process. In Britain, applicants can have unlimited attempts at the theory test (Part 1) element of the qualifying tests and applicants must pass Parts 2 and 3 within two years of passing Part 1.  If they fail Part 2 or Part 3 three times, or if their two-year validity on Part 1 expires, the applicant must restart the entire process.  However, before they can restart at Part 1 they must wait until 2 years have passed since they previously passed Part 1. However, here an applicant has a maximum of three attempts at each element (Part 1, Part 2 and Part 3).  If they fail any element three times, or their Part 1 two-year validity expires, they must restart the process.  Restarting here means the applicant must wait for two years after they first sat their Part 1.Road safety continues to be of paramount importance for my Department, and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. As my resources are limited, I will continue to focus them on interventions where evidence shows they will have the greatest impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (a) the number of Assembly Questions for Written Answer her Department has received; and (b) the total number of members correspondence her Department and the Northern Ireland Prison Service has received, in relation to animals in prisons since 1 August 2025; (ii) to detail the amount of time her officials have spent responding to these Assembly Questions for Written Answer and items of correspondence; and (iii) for an estimate of the cost of carrying out this work.
answer_text: Since 1 August 2025, the Department and the Northern Ireland Prison Service has received 2 oral questions, 25 Assembly Questions for Written Answer and 9 correspondence cases from members in relation to animals in prison. It is estimated that officials have spent a total of 232 hours responding to these.It has not been possible to accurately estimate the cost of this work, but we can be confident that it is considerable, and I believe that is deeply regrettable in the context of the severe financial challenges facing Northern Ireland’s justice system. Those who proport to be advocates for better outcomes in the justice system should reflect on the role they are playing in draining its resources in this way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's next steps following the publication of three cervical screening related reports by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: It is clear from the work undertaken to date, including the recently published reports, that there were issues with the Southern Trust’s laboratory cervical cytology service, including screener performance and their management. There were also issues relating to the Quality Assurance function which the Public Health Agency provided. Both the Trust and the PHA have apologised for these failings and the harm they caused. While the recently published reports have provided further clarity and answers, some of the findings in the reports do not necessarily align with previously published reports. This does not mean that the findings set out in any one report are taken account of more, or less so, than the findings in the other reports but reflects that cervical screening is complex and can be quite technical. I have asked Professor Sir Frank Atherton, previously the Chief Medical Officer in Wales, to undertake a review of all of the work completed to date. Professor Sir Frank Atherton is completely independent of the cervical screening programme in Northern Ireland, and he will provide his expert advice directly to me, independent of the Trust, PHA and my Department. It is expected his work will be completed in early 2026. I must stress that the cervical screening programme has changed over the last few years. The primary test in the current pathway is now HPV, which is then followed by cytology for those samples that test positive for HPV, this pathway is expected to find 90% of abnormalities; there is one regional laboratory, and the PHA quality assurance role has been augmented. I would urge all women to come forward for screening when they are invited to do so. Screening saves lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's next steps following the expert review in early 2026 of all the published reports related to the Southern Health and Social Care Trust Cervical Cytology Review.
answer_text: It is clear from the work undertaken to date, including the recently published reports, that there were issues with the Southern Trust’s laboratory cervical cytology service, including screener performance and their management.  There were also issues relating to the Quality Assurance function which the Public Health Agency provided.  Both the Trust and the PHA have apologised for these failings and the harm they caused.While the recently published reports have provided further clarity and answers, some of the findings in the reports do not necessarily align with previously published reports. This does not mean that the findings set out in any one report are taken account of more, or less so, than the findings in the other reports but reflects that cervical screening is complex and can be quite technical. I have asked Professor Sir Frank Atherton, previously the Chief Medical Officer in Wales, to undertake a review of all of the work completed to date.  Professor Sir Frank Atherton is completely independent of the cervical screening programme in Northern Ireland, and he will provide his expert advice directly to me, independent of the Trust, PHA and my Department. It is expected his work will be completed in early 2026 and I will then consider his findings and advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to develop Mandeville Industrial Estate, Craigavon.
answer_text: Through its Regional Property Programme, Invest NI is progressing plans for the development of Mandeville Business Park in Craigavon. This will begin with the installation of essential infrastructure to enable initial access. In line with the approach taken for other business parks under its ownership, development will be phased and guided by business demand. This provides flexibility in how and when additional phases are delivered, ensuring a balance between current and future needs while safeguarding the responsible use of public funds.Invest NI will shortly appoint an Integrated Consultancy Team to finalise the site design plans. Once completed, these plans will be submitted for consideration through the statutory planning process.My Department remains firmly committed to supporting balanced economic growth across the North. The delivery of Mandeville Business Park will play an important role in achieving this aim.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to develop the 24 per cent undeveloped land bank, held by Invest NI in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area.
answer_text: Invest NI’s industrial land in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough plays a vital role in enabling business growth. Most of this land has already been successfully developed by businesses to deliver on their growth ambitions.  One of the largest remaining landholdings is at Mandeville in Craigavon, where Invest NI is advancing plans for future development through its Regional Property Programme.  Initial plans will focus on installing essential infrastructure to provide access, followed by phased development guided by business demand. This approach ensures flexibility, balances current and future needs, and safeguards the responsible use of public funds.Design work for the site is scheduled to commence shortly, after which plans will be submitted for consideration through the statutory planning process.Invest NI remains committed to working closely with local stakeholders to identify property requirements and deliver solutions that support long-term economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what initiatives his Department is implementing to support staff recruitment and retention in respite care facilities, in order to restore services to pre-COVID-19 pandemic levels.
answer_text: My Department works closely with the HSC to restore services to pre Covid -19 pandemic levels in line with the Health and Social Care NI Reset Plan. Within the Reset Plan, workforce stabilisation is a priority. My health officials work closely with each of the Trusts, each of whom have provided a response to supporting staff recruitment and retention in respite care facilities.Southern TrustThe Southern Trust continue to ensure timely recruitment to vacant posts, both full time and temporary (including maternity) within their short breaks unit and endeavour to maintain a cohort of bank staff to offer support during times of increased sickness and absence. The Trust are currently in the process of recruiting an Intellectual Disability Psychology lead who will provide support to staff within their residential short breaks unit supporting the delivery of a reflective practice framework as part of the wider model of therapeutic care.  The Trust are also actively recruiting Allied Health Professional staff to work alongside the core short breaks teams to support them in their care of young people availing of overnight short breaks.The Trust ensures robust governance arrangements are in place, and that all staff receive regular supervision via professional lines with appraisal also being undertaken on an annual basis.  Appropriate training is available during the year for staff with all new staff receiving an induction into their roles.The volume of short breaks for children and young people has increased from the pre COVID position.Northern Health and Social Care TrustTo support improved performance in the area of staff recruitment the NHSCT has employed a Workforce Co-ordinator to ensure timely recruitment to all posts within Community Wellbeing Division. The Trust has commissioned an advertising company to assist with recruitment, to include advertisements on billboards, bus shelters and social media platforms. With respect to retention of staff the NHSCT has a number of supports in place: good supervisionmaintenance of adequate staffing levelsregular team meetingsteam building eventsreflective groupscoaching and mentoring for managersservice workshops and training for staffregular senior manager engagement with teams South Eastern TrustThe Southern Trust has recently carried out successful dedicated recruitment campaigns for both Short Breaks and Long-term Residential Care Homes. The campaigns included the use of media platforms, social/local media and local poster campaigns. Staff have personally advocated the benefits and impact of their jobs through storytelling narratives on video clips to share with potential applicants. Bespoke recruitment opportunities have been held that have allowed the Trust to recruit in a timelier manner to essential posts than afforded by conventional recruitment processes. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following confirmation that £500,000 has been allocated for the Craigavon Area Hospital redevelopment programme team, healthcare planning, and site master plan, to outline when the funding will be released to progress the planning phase of the redevelopment.
answer_text: The Southern Health and Social Care Trust continues to develop the long-term plans for the site redevelopment programme at Craigavon Area Hospital (CAH).  Due to the scale of the programme the project will be broken down into phases, and the Trust is currently refining the preferred phased redevelopment approach at CAH. The substantial projected cost of the programme means that affordability will be a key issue, in the context of the challenging financial environment and the scale of demand for other capital investment projects in the Health and Social Care sector.Within a briefing to the Health Committee in March 2025 the Department advised of the costs to progress projects in design and development, including the redevelopment of CAH. In light of the financial implications for future years it has not been possible to commit funding to some of the projects in the current financial year, including the redevelopment of CAH. The future of these projects will instead be considered as part of the development of the capital investment plan.Following the announcement of the outcome of the UK wide Spending Review 2025 in June, the Department of Finance commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30. My ability to commit to funding capital projects, including the CAH redevelopment project, will depend on my Department’s capital allocation from the Budget process. The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the Department of Health capital investment plan which will set out the projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the cost breakdown of the nine Special Educational Needs Transformation projects to be funded by the £27.5 million received from the Interim Public Services Transformation Board.
answer_text: The nine Special Educational Needs (SEN) Transformation projects to be funded from the ring-fenced funding of £27.5m from the Transformation funding are focused on two strands:Strand 1 - ‘Right Support at the Right Time for Children & Young People & Parents/Families’ – focuses on early intervention and includes seven projects to be developed and trialled which will test outcomes for children with emerging SEN, as follows:Transforming the Statutory Assessment Process;Transformation of Support Model;Establish and deliver a pilot Early Childhood SEN Intervention Programme;Establish and deliver a pilot Speech, Language & Communications Intervention Toolkit & Programme;Transforming Inclusive Play Environments;Adopt and trial the Early Intervention Nurture Programme into an Enhanced ‘Nurture Plus’ Programme;Special Schools as Resource Centres of Expertise (pilot).Strand 2 – ‘Right People’ – focuses on building a skilled and confident workforce and includes two pilots:Design & Trial a Teacher & Classroom Assistant Professional Learning Programme;Design & Trial an Inclusive Capacity Building Programme. The costings for each project are indicative and subject to change as the projects progress, with some subject to final proposals from delivery partners.  The working detail of costings are not planned for publication; however, there will be full transparency and accountability on expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the impact of Remote Evidence Centres.
answer_text: AQO 2519 22-27 - ANSWER Remote Evidence Centres, or ‘RECs’, allow vulnerable witnesses to give evidence directly to the court from a secure and comfortable facility, away from the courthouse. Importantly, attending a REC ensures that the witness will not encounter the defendant or the defendant’s family or supporters in or around the court building on the day of the trial.   My Department has established two new RECs in Belfast and Craigavon. These new facilities are in addition to the existing NSPCC-led facility in Derry ~ Londonderry.   We measure the impact of RECs in three main ways – usage, case outcomes and feedback from users.  In terms of usage, my officials have worked hard to increase awareness and promote the use of the RECs, with considerable success. In 2024, there was an increase of over 600% in terms of trial bookings compared to 2023 and this increase in demand has continued in 2025, with a further 90% increase in bookings so far this year compared to 2024. In the whole of 2024, 344 vulnerable victims and witnesses attended a REC to give evidence; so far this year, that number stands at 431.   In terms of case outcomes, the conviction rate for REC cases in 2024 was 92%, and so far this year, the rate stands at over 94%. These figures compare favourably to the conviction rate in criminal cases when the complainant attends the courthouse to give evidence.  There are several reasons why the conviction rate has remained high, including, when the complainant is offered the opportunity to attend a REC, they almost always turn up on the day of the trial, something that is not always the case when they are required to attend the courthouse. In addition, many witnesses have told us they feel more confident, secure and relaxed at the REC, which enables them to give their best evidence.  Feedback from those who have used the REC is universally positive with many victims telling us that without it, they would not have been able to give evidence or indeed, continue with their case at all.  In summary then, REC usage continues to increase; case outcomes are very encouraging and those who have used the REC tell us they have made an incredibly positive difference to their experience of the justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of complex wound dressings following the announcement by GPs in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust that they are removing this care from their treatment room services.
answer_text: I regret that it was not possible to secure agreement with the NI General Practitioners Committee on this year’s contract and their subsequent decision to take collective action.  The 2025/26 GMS contract included an additional £9.5m investment in General Practice. In the context of the inadequate budget allocated to my Department by the Executive, and the extremely challenging financial position the Department faces, this represented the best possible offer that could be made.  In addition to the 2025/26 GMS contract, my Department has announced investment of a further £61m into the Primary Care MDT programme over the next 5 years, which will expand the range of services available in Primary Care and help address the high levels of demand being dealt with by our GP practices, as well as £2.9m into the GP Elective Service which will increase the levels of service being provided in important primary care services including the Gynae service. I have also accepted pay recommendations for 2025/26 from the relevant bodies and have issued a Ministerial Direction instructing their immediate implementation. The Ministerial Direction has been referred to the Executive for approval. Taken together, total investment in General Practice in 2025/26 would be £414m.My priority has been, and continues to be, to ensure that patients can continue to access safe, high quality, sustainable GP services when they need to.  This continues to apply during collective action.  GPs must continue to deliver upon their contractual commitments as they take this action and ensure that impact on patients is minimised. Any action that is taken must respect contractual obligations, protect patient safety, and maintain service access. My Department has written to GPs to remind them of their responsibilities on these issues and will closely monitor adherence to ensure that practices continue to deliver to their contractual requirements. The Trust is accepting the referrals for wound care to be delivered in an alternative clinical setting or potentially in the patient’s own home, processes are currently being put in place to ensure a smooth transition.  The Trust will be monitoring the work flow for this service.In terms of access to GP services more widely, the General Medical Services (GMS) contract for 2025/26, introduced a range of measures which, while relatively small in scale, will make a tangible improvement to the patient experience of accessing GP services.  These measures do not seek to increase the number of consultations delivered nor to make GPs work to unsafe levels.  Rather, they aim to create small but significant improvement to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike.  In many cases, these are things that practices here are already doing and the aim has been to seek to spread that good practice across the region.Looking to the future of General Practice,

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Investment Strategy remains under consideration and we hope to be able to bring it to the Executive in the near future. The timeline for approval and publication of the Strategy will then be a matter for the Executive.The draft Investment Strategy – ‘Infrastructure 2050’ – sets out our vision and strategic objectives over the next 25 years, alongside a 10-year Investment Plan.The Strategy aims to stabilise and transform infrastructure investment in key areas such as our schools, hospitals, transport, and digital connectivity. At the same time, it will recognise the need for regional balance.We know that, just as in other jurisdictions, major capital projects have experienced delays and overspend. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of whether job advertisements in (i) the Northern Ireland Civil Service; and (ii) North-South bodies, where applicable, should include the requirement for a degree or demonstrably equivalent experience, rather than degree only.
answer_text: The Civil Service is committed to fair and open recruitment practices that support equality of opportunity and attract a diverse applicant pool. The requirement for a degree or other formal qualification will only be specified if it is essential to the job role being recruited, for example, in roles such as accountancy or engineering, where professional accreditation is governed by external regulatory bodies. For all other job roles in the Civil Service, eligibility requirements focus on the skills, experience, and behaviours required to effectively deliver the duties of the role which are assessed through the Civil Service Competency Framework.The Civil Service is not responsible for recruitment processes for North-South bodies and therefore I cannot comment on eligibility requirements for its job roles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the revised Civil Service policy which requires at least 40 per cent of working hours to be spent in the office.
answer_text: The Civil Service Hybrid Working policy was implemented in June 2022. When developed, it was agreed the policy should be regularly reviewed to ensure it meets the needs of the Civil Service.The Civil Service remains committed to fostering a modern and inclusive workplace by offering flexible working options, including hybrid arrangements, to support both efficient and cost-effective public service delivery.A recent review of the Hybrid Working Policy reaffirms the value of flexible working while also acknowledging the significant advantages of workplace attendance.The updated Hybrid Working Policy will be implemented across all departments from 1 September to 31 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the timeline to implement the recommendations from the All-Island Strategic Rail Review Report.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review includes a roadmap which sets out a potential timeline for the delivery of interventions across three time horizons: short-term interventions to be delivered by 2030, medium-term interventions to be delivered between 2030 and 2040, and long-term interventions to be delivered between 2040 and 2050.The Department continues to work with our colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Iarnród Éireann and NTA, with the assistance of European Investment Bank consultants, to consider the recommendations in the Review and develop a plan for implementation. This work is expected to continue over the next number of months. Translink are also undertaking several feasibility studies, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which are due to report in the coming months.  The outcomes from these studies and the work with our southern colleagues will help inform my prioritisation of projects to progress as funding opportunities arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the consideration she has given to the recommendation from the All-Island Strategic Rail Review Report to build a new direct line between Lisburn and Newry, which would provide rail services in Banbridge.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review recommended that consideration be given to developing a new railway line from Belfast to Newry via Hillsborough, Dromore, and Banbridge, with connections to the Lisburn-Antrim line and towards Portadown.  This has been identified as a long-term intervention by the Review i.e. to be delivered between 2040 and 2050, however, more work is needed to test the feasibility and affordability of the Review’s recommendations, determine an optimum sequencing for the delivery of projects, and secure the necessary funding to take projects forward. The Department is continuing to work with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink,  Iarnród Éireannand NTA to consider the recommendations in the Review and develop a plan for implementation.  I will then need to consider which projects to prioritise as funding opportunities become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the actions her Department has taken to progress the recommendations within the All-Island Strategic Rail Review Report.
answer_text: The Department of Transport has secured support from the European Investment Bank (EIB) Advisory Services to help progress implementation of the Rail Review’s recommendations. With the assistance of EIB consultants, my officials are working with colleagues in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail, and the National Transport Authority to consider the Review’s recommendations. This work has included the preparation of a Project Prioritisation Strategy, which identifies those rail interventions which could be well advanced or delivered over the next decade to 2035. The EIB consultant team are currently focused on conducting a more detailed analysis of key major projects. Following completion of this work, the draft Project Prioritisation Strategy, which has been broadly agreed by the project Steering Group, will be reviewed and published later this year.  My officials are also working closely with Translink who are undertaking several feasibility studies, linked to recommendations in the Rail Review, which are due to report in the coming months. These include studies on reopening the Lisburn-Antrim line, with a link to Belfast International Airport; reinstating the Portadown-Armagh line; a new line from Portadown to Derry; and electrification of Belfast to the border.The outcomes from these studies and the work with our southern colleagues will help inform my prioritisation of projects to progress as funding opportunities arise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered reviewing the policy on enabling GPs to issue a NHS prescription based on private consultations.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of their individual patients.Individuals choosing to procure healthcare privately are effectively stepping outside of the health and social care (HSC) system.  In these circumstances, there is no obligation on GPs to provide pre and/or post treatment support.  Therefore, it remains important for individuals wishing to avail of private healthcare to ensure that all pre and post treatment support costs are included in the overall package of care procured from the private healthcare provider.  In instances where any ongoing treatment and medication is recommended, but is not provided, by the private provider as part of the private service then individuals should be prepared to meet any associated costs.GPs remain responsible for clinical decisions regarding the care of individual patients and as such they should exercise their clinical judgement prior to providing any pre and/or post treatment support for individuals availing of private healthcare.Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to provide such support, in instances where a GP considers that the support being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their GMS contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) standards on prescribing and Good Medical Practice then they may choose to provide this support or continue to provide this support.However, there may be additional factors to consider in specific circumstances where a GP is being asked to provide an HSC prescription to a patient based on the recommendation of a private practitioner. If a GP considers that ongoing prescribing of a medicine is appropriate for supply through the HSC service, they are content that the treatment falls within their clinical remit and that if necessary appropriate shared care arrangements have been agreed with the private practitioner, the HSC prescriber and the patient/carers, then under the HSC terms of service they may issue an HSC prescription.  In reaching this decision the GP should apply the same criteria had the request originated within an HSC clinic, such as consideration of the Northern Ireland Formulary and national recommendations.By accepting the advice of the private practitioner and proceeding to sign the prescription, the GP is accepting clinical responsibility for this decision.There are currently no plans to review the Department’s position.  The principle in which individuals procuring private healthcare are effectively steeping outside the HSC remains appropriate.  Furthermore, there is a clinical and legal responsibility on the prescriber as they must be able to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 26183/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline whether his Department will consider funding accredited online schools for children of compulsory school age who cannot attend the traditional school setting, given (i) the growing demand for Education Otherwise Than at School and (ii) the shortage of available places.
answer_text: The Department recognises the challenges many young people face to remain engaged in formal education, particularly in terms of social, emotional, behavioural and wellbeing issues which can make sustaining a place in a mainstream school setting difficult.Education Otherwise Than at School (EOTAS) is an educational provision for young people with social, emotional, behavioural or other difficulties who cannot sustain a place in a mainstream school setting and who, without significant levels of support, will struggle to remain engaged in formal education.  It ensures that they can continue to access learning until they are either able to return to mainstream or until they reach compulsory school leaving age. 	The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, Article 86 (1) requires the EA make arrangements for the provision of suitable education for those children of compulsory school age who, by reason of illness, or otherwise, cannot attend school.While the department does not currently fund online schools for children of compulsory school age, officials are keen to explore the provision of alternative education provision more broadly through the recently announced RAISE Cross- Cutting Initiative “Alternative Education Models Pilot”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how forward planning for special educational needs provision will ensure continuity of support from primary to post-primary education in Upper Bann, where there are significantly more places at primary level for children with special educational needs and disabilities than at post-primary level.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The Education Authority (EA) set out the strategic direction on how the future educational needs of children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) will be addressed through area solutions across the region, consistent with the relevant Ministerial Priorities in the Special Education Strategic Area Plan 2022-27: Planning for Special Education Provision, details of which can be found at: Strategic Area Plans 2022-27 | Education Authority Northern IrelandThe aim of area planning is to ensure that all primary and post primary pupils have access to a broad and balanced curriculum that meets their educational needs in a school that is educationally and financially viable and sustainable. Thereby providing a network of schools that are the right type, the right size and located in the right place at the right time, with a focus on raising standards.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered giving cancer patients access to free dental care due to the side effects of cancer treatment on their oral health.
answer_text: Some cancer treatments can have a negative impact on the mouth and preventive interventions designed to counteract the increased risk of dental decay and periodontal disease. Close monitoring of oral health and early disease intervention are key elements in the oral health management of patients undergoing cancer treatment. It can be beneficial for patients to see a dentist for examination and any necessary treatment prior to starting cancer treatment.  However, there are currently no plans to introduce free dental care for cancer patients. Entitlement to free dental care is outlined in the Dental Charges Regulations (Northern Ireland). While this does not include cancer patients they may be eligible under other criteria or be able to get help with the costs under the Low Income Scheme.Details on entitlement to free General Dental Services can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/health-service-dental-charges-and-treatments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her plans to introduce a Sentencing Bill.
To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration his Department has given to the need for a women’s refuge provision within the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The Supporting People (SP) programme which is administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, provides grant funding towards the provision of housing related support services which help vulnerable people living independently in the community. This provision includes refuges for women affected by domestic violence. With regard to refuge accommodation, there is currently no SP funded refuge located directly within the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area, however refuge provision is available in nearby areas. This includes facilities in Newry (delivered by Women’s Aid Armagh Down), Lisburn (delivered by Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid), and Cookstown (delivered by Causeway & Mid Ulster Women’s Aid). Access is determined by risk and need and is not restricted by council boundaries.SP fund Women's Aid Armagh Down to deliver a Floating Support service, which covers the entire ABC Council area, providing community-based support to women, and women with children, who are experiencing or who are at risk of domestic abuse. At a strategic level, the SP Strategic Plan and COVID Recovery Plan 2022–2025 recognises the importance of domestic violence provision and includes specific actions aimed at service development. While this commitment is reflected in the strategy, it should be noted that the expansion of provision is contingent on the availability of additional funding. Proposals for new or extended services funded under the SP Programme are also required to progress through the established commissioning structures.Addressing domestic violence provision remains a priority within the SP Programme, and we continue to work closely with partners to ensure available resources are targeted where they are most needed’.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

answer_text: Since its inception in 2017, the Investment Fund has made loans totalling almost £170 million, which in turn has leveraged a further £170 million in private sector investment. It has helped to finance projects across a range of sectors and localities. In doing so, the Fund has supported over 5,100 jobs, the development of 15.9 hectares of brownfield land and over 99,000 square metres of employment space. While I am keen that the Fund continues to do more to support projects that bring about economic and social regeneration, I believe that, to this point, it has been an effective vehicle for the deployment of Financial Transactions Capital to development projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the average time to complete a feasibility assessment for an electric vehicle charging hub.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points and hubs is a commercially led operation in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of those charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Individual CPOs are best placed to advise on their individual electric vehicle infrastructure plans and associated timescales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting times for sleep studies to diagnose sleep apnea in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any measures his Department is taking to prevent underage use of sunbeds; and (ii) whether he will consider increasing the frequency of spot checks in sunbed premises, which are currently bi-annual.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with partner organisations, including the Public Health Agency, District Councils and Cancer Focus NI, to raise awareness of the harms of the sun and UV rays, and is committed to ensuring all sunbed premises in Northern Ireland operate in accordance with the current sunbed legislation. To spread awareness of the age restrictions and the risk of sunbed use guidance has been made widely available for sunbed owners along with sunbeds health warning posters and information leaflets for sunbed users. Environmental Health Officers in District Councils collaborate through a Sunbeds Task Group to oversee and enforce the sunbeds legislation in NI and each council has committed to regularly inspect sunbed premises across their areas on a bi-annual basis. This includes carrying out test purchases to ascertain if those operating sunbed establishments are allowing minors, under 18 years of age, to use sunbeds and if identification (ID) checks are being made. Councils have powers to act where there is evidence of underage use of sunbeds. Last year councils served 18 fixed penalty notices for non-compliance with the sunbed legislation and in 2023, 28 fixed penalty notices were issued. There is no immediate plan to increase the number of spot checks however if the councils receive a complaint from the public e.g. a school reports that pupils under 18 have been accessing a particular sunbed premises, the council may pay a regulatory visit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department is supporting local businesses in response to the trade tariffs announce by President Trump.
answer_text: Earlier this week I met with business and Union representatives from across the North to understand better the potential impact of the tariff announcements – I will have further such meetings. I also met with the British Business Secretary and the Tánaiste to advocate for local business and will continue to do so as the situation evolves to ensure the best possible outcomes for our economy. At a practical level, Invest NI is also on hand to assist businesses as they navigate the new trading reality, including an upcoming webinar on how to export and import goods under the new tariff measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when construction work will commence for the extension and nursery unit of Lurgan Model Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: Lurgan Model Integrated Primary School was announced in May 2018 to advance in design under the Second Call to the School Enhancement Programme.  On 28 May 2024 I announced funding to progress the project to construction.The Education Authority is currently preparing a business case addendum for the project and subject to approval of this by my Department, the EA will commence the procurement process to appoint a contractor to construct the works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when a baby loss certificate scheme will be introduced.
answer_text: ANSWERI am keen to see this initiative continue to be taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill, which will provide a power to make regulations to form a scheme, has completed its First and Second Stages in the Assembly.Work will follow – involving officials from the Department of Health – to consult with the stakeholders on the detail of the scheme, to draft the required secondary legislation and to operationalise these processes.If these matters progress smoothly, I anticipate that the scheme may be operational by the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22930/22-27, whether she has considered Shared Island funding for the Bannfoot pedestrian bridge.
answer_text: The Bannfoot pedestrian bridge is being taken forward as a local Active Travel project to improve pedestrian and cycle links around Lough Neagh as part of the Loughshore Trail.  Shared Island funding would not be required for delivery of this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the process for allocating Minor Capital Works.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on funding for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: As of week commencing 24 March 2025, there have been 1,993 applications determined as recommended for payment, resulting in over £83 million paid to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications.  1,031 applications have been determined as not eligible for payment.The position of the Executive remains, that the Scheme, which was designed and legislated for by the Westminster Government, goes further than originally intended by the Executive and that not enough consideration was given to the potential impact on the Executive’s budget. The Finance Minister continues to progress financial discussions with the Westminster Government in the context of their funding responsibilities for the Scheme.On 12 April 2021 the then First Minister, deputy First Minister and Finance Minister provided an undertaking to the Court that payments will be made to successful applicants under the Scheme.  The undertaking provides reassurance and confidence that payments will be made when they fall due under the terms of the Scheme, regardless of where the funding comes from.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the purchase of land at Mandeville Road North, Craigavon, will be completed to enable the development of Portadown Integrated Nursery and Primary School.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to the current constraints on my Department’s capital budget the purchase of this land cannot be progressed at this time, and regrettably I am not in a position to confirm when the purchase will take place. I will however continue to make a strong case for increased and sustained funding for Education, to permit this and many other much needed school build projects to proceed to construction as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's criteria for prioritisation of school capital projects.
answer_text: The Department’s school capital programme funds Special Educational Needs (SEN) places as well as maintenance, minor works and all new school builds and extensions. The school funding streams criteria are listed below. School Enhancement Programme (SEP)The second call under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP2) for projects opened in January 2017 with a closure date of 28 February 2017.  All applications to the programme were assessed under the published protocol which details the selection criteria and is available at School Enhancement Programme - Second Call Protocol for selection | Department of EducationFunding continues to be available to progress SEP2 projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.Major Capital Works The criteria for selection of Major Capital Works projects are set out in the Departments “Protocols for Selection of Major Capital Works” document. The Protocol for the most recent call for major works, announced in 2022, is available at    https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/topics/protocol-selection-major-capital-works-december-2021 Due to the current capital budget position and number of schools already on the major works programme, I do not intend to make a further call for major works in the near future.   Special Educational Needs (SEN)In April 2024 I created a standalone capital programme to support children with SEN. All SEN capital projects across all other project streams were brought under the one dedicated programme, with its own budget, to ensure the works are prioritised and expedited for delivery. Minor WorksWithin the current budget constraints, minor works are restricted to Ministerial Priority 1 i.e. those works that meet inescapable statutory requirements, such as health and safety, fire protection, safeguarding and statutory obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act. Schemes which meet those requirements are prioritised for delivery, although the timescales for delivery will vary depending on issues like: Their state of readiness e.g. need for planning approval.The logistics of delivery e.g. some projects can only be delivered when pupils are not on site, limiting the window to holiday periods, typically the summer months. The availability of funding e.g. some small-scale projects can be “mopped up” at the end of the financial year if there is available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the business case for the Lurgan signalling improvement project.
answer_text: The signalling system in the Lurgan area is approaching the end of its serviceable life. Consequently, in March 2025 Translink submitted a revised Outline Business Case for a new signalling project for the Lurgan area to my Department.  This is currently being assessed by my officials and will commence following the necessary approvals and the availability of funding. The new signalling system would reduce the impact of the crossing closure times across the three level crossings in Lurgan, as well reducing the likelihood of public misuse at the crossings. It could also facilitate more and faster train services on this line.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on compensation payments following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Question 13 AQO 1822/22-27Following disruption and damage caused by Storm Éowyn on 24th of January, a working group involving the Department for the Economy, NIE Networks, and the Utility Regulator, was established to consider compensation payments for electricity customers most affected by power outages. The working group was established at the request of the Executive to consider options where compensation payments would be recovered through customer electricity bills. The working group has now updated the Executive of its findings that there is currently no existing route to make payments to customers. NIE Networks has applied a severe weather exemption provided for in legislation. The working group concluded that any other options involving recovery of payments from network charges would require legal or regulatory modifications which cannot be applied retrospectively for those customers impacted by Storm Éowyn.UR has agreed to include a commitment in the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2025, that they will consult again on the Electricity Guaranteed Standards of Service, including on arrangements in response to severe weather events. Once they have presented their conclusions to the Department, we will process any required change to the Regulations aiming for new Regulations in 2025/26.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his engagement with the UK Government on a sanitary and phytosanitary Veterinary Agreement with the European Union.
answer_text: I continue to take every opportunity to engage with the UK Government on this matter because it is critical that they negotiate with the EU for an SPS agreement that works for the whole of the UK in removing trade friction. I would therefore be supportive of a SPS agreement that is as comprehensive as possible and ideally cover all the SPS legislation in Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework and resolve the position on veterinary medicines. Where Northern Ireland is aligned with EU Regulations, it is desirable that GB is also aligned as that means common regulations across the UK and the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she will update the statutory remit of the Utility Regulator to include (i) decarbonisation; and (ii) climate change targets in its duties.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator is an independent body which plays an integral role in supporting DfE’s decarbonisation journey.   My Department is contributing to government’s statutory targets under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 by developing new energy policies and legislation. Each piece of new legislation will include the necessary statutory powers that the Utility Regulator requires to enable it to carry out any new regulatory duties. For example, my Department has consulted on a new Utility Regulator (Support for Decarbonisation Preparation) Bill which is designed to support development of net-zero energy policies, and is progressing legislation that will enable the delivery of a new Renewable Electricity Support Scheme, which will include new powers for the regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Northern Ireland Water's installation of Event Duration Monitors.
answer_text: NI Water intends to install 646 event duration monitors in PC21, at an approximate cost of £16.5m.The installation of these event duration monitors has been planned in three two-year phases and commenced in April 2021, with the PC21 (Phase 1) focusing on bathing and shellfish water locations and subsequent phases focusing on other priority areas, as agreed with NIEA. The EDMs for PC21 (Phase 1) are fully installed and the data has substantially completed validation, with the majority now confirmed to be sending reliable information back to NI Water’s Alarm Management Centre.In PC21 (Phase 2), NI Water has progressed installation of EDMs in other priority areas and overflows, as agreed with NIEA, and the data is undergoing validation.As of October 2024, 292 storm overflows have had EDMs installed and sufficiently verified to support effective use for operational maintenance and monitoring. Of these, 122 have been fully quality assured for both operational maintenance and release of spill count data to the publicNI Water is working with NIEA on progressing Phase 3 in 2026-27, which will include further deployments within the Lough Neagh area.NI Water also plans to install a further 98 EDMs at Wastewater Treatment Works during PC21 and is working with NIEA regarding priority sites. The information provided by event duration monitors will give NI Water an accurate assessment of how discharge points are performing and will help to inform future investment plans, ensuring money is targeted where most needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the submission of the business case for the new Gender Identity Service.
answer_text: Following significant engagement between my Department and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, a detailed business case for the Lifespan Gender Service was submitted on 6 March 2025. My officials are now considering its contents, including funding requirement, and will shortly provide me with advice for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will review the current process for allocating funding to schools for minor capital works.
answer_text: My Department’s internal audit carried out a review of the Minor Works processes for non-controlled schools in May 2023. One of the objectives of that review was “To ensure that schemes within the minor works programme are prioritised to ensure effective use of the available capital budget”. The audit opinion was that, overall, there is a satisfactory system of governance, risk management and control. In October 2017 the Department increased the Education Authority’s (EA) capital delegation from £500k to £1m. This effectively meant that from that date, all EA capital projects below that threshold are carried out by the EA without reference to the Department.The EA is responsible for determining its own minor works priorities and for planning, managing and carrying out these works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the ownership of the historic Chapel Lock on the Lagan Navigational Canal to the north of Aghagallon.
