question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why waiting times have increased for patients with haemochromatosis requiring regular venesection; and (ii) what steps his Department is taking to ensure that patients receive timely and appropriate treatment to prevent complications.
answer_text: Haemochromatosis care in Northern Ireland is provided by a range of specialists in hepatology, gastroenterology and haematology in Trusts as well as care professionals in Primary Care.  The condition is genetic and caused by excessive iron in the blood. Management is through regular venesection (bloodletting)(i) Patients, once diagnosed with Haemochromatosis, require regular monitoring and venesection.  This is delivered by Trusts through ambulatory care services and the Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service.  Unfortunately waiting times have increased for treatment due to limited funded capacity and an increase in demand.(ii) Diagnosis of Genetic Haemochromotosis follows a GP referral to a specialist in secondary care and attendance at an outpatient appointment.  In the initial stages of treatment, most patients must attend hospital for venesection until they achieve a lower target level of ferritin.  Once this level is attained, patients require regular hospital appointments every three or four months for venesection to retain the lower ferritin levels.  In recent years however, suitable patients have now been given the opportunity to have maintenance venesections through the NI Blood Transfusion Service along with less frequent hospital appointments for monitoring.   This has the benefit of releasing outpatient appointments as well as increasing blood donations.   The Belfast Trust is currently exploring opportunities to increase the number of Haemochromotosis venesections through NIBTS but this requires additional resources not yet available and stringent governance processes around monitoring.In addition, UK blood and transplant regulations unfortunately limit the frequency with which NIBTS can offer treatment to patients and are only able to offer venesection every six weeks rather than every fortnight. This is inconsistent with best practice guidelines and limits the number of newly diagnosed patients who can avail of donating blood to NIBTS. Venesections at mobile units can only be provided at scheduled mobile sessions that may not match the regularity required for the patients’ treatment.Trusts continue to prioritise referrals at triage and have also continued to validate waiting lists and prioritise additional funding when this has been identified.  A limited number of GPs provide a venesection service. Although this is currently only in one locality, GPs are encouraged to take up the voluntary scheme and so provide a local service for patients.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the contingency plans that were in place prior to Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The NI Civil Contingencies Framework – Building Resilience Together, was launched in 2021 and reviewed in 2023. At the regional level, it lays out arrangements for effective emergency management for preparing for, responding to, and recovering from an emergency across Government, Local Government and responding agencies. it is underpinned by operational plans, owned by each Department which are specific for emergency events.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they had with the UK Government for mutual aid assistance in advance of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The requirement for mutual aid is a key consideration during the prepare phase of any emergency.  Mutual Aid can take many forms, such as through financial, logistical or resourcing support and can be provided via commercial, government or charitable / voluntary agreements. NIE Networks, as members of the North, East, South, West Area Consortium (NEWSAC), commenced conversations with GB counterparts in advance of the Storm arriving. This is agreed and implemented on an organisation-to-organisation commercial basis.As the severity of the storm impacts developed, we engaged with both the UK and Irish Governments on potential sources of mutual aid assistance. With support from COBR Unit, we were able to secure additional resources for NIE Networks at short notice.  NI Water made requests via the WaterUK mutual agreement for additional bottled water supplies. The Department for Infrastructure made initial requests for additional resources via COBR, but as they progressed their response efforts these were not needed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps his Department is taking, along with all relevant agencies, to ensure Abercorn Primary School, Banbridge, is reopened as soon as possible following the impact of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The Education Authority worked closely with relevant agencies to ensure all schools could safely reopen as quickly as possible after Storm Éowyn. As the power restoration date for Abercorn Primary School was initially identified as 1 February, the school received a generator on the evening of Tuesday 28 January. However, an earthing issue during installation of the generator resulted in the school remaining closed on Wednesday 29 January. NIE engineers resolved the earthing fault, and the school reopened on Thursday 30 January, powered by the generator. Mains power to the school was restored on 31 January.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale for his decision to reject an application by Bridge Integrated Primary School, Banbridge, to establish a nursery unit.
answer_text: I have thoroughly reviewed and considered Development Proposal (DP) 714 for Bridge Integrated Primary School (IPS), along with all the related documents and information provided.  I have also taken into account DP 732, which proposes the transformation of Downshire Nursery School into a Controlled Integrated School.  These two schools are located very close to each other, and many children who attend Downshire Nursery School go on to Bridge IPS or other nearby schools.  Although these proposals are separate, they both focus on the development and expansion of integrated pre-school education in the Banbridge area.The data analysis indicates that there is already adequate provision of pre-school education in the area, making it unnecessary to add more, especially considering the risk of displacing existing quality pre-school providers if this proposal were approved.There are significant costs associated with this proposal, and with population projections showing a decline in the number of pre-school-aged children in both the short and long term, approving this would not be a responsible use of the limited funds available for education.  I am also conscious of the statutory obligation under Article 44 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 and the principles of Managing Public Money, which require the effective and efficient use of public resources.I recognise that this is the third proposal submitted by the school for a nursery unit and understand the disappointment this decision may cause. However, the approval of the transformation of Downshire Nursery School to Controlled Integrated Status, with 52 full-time places, will sufficiently meet the demand from parents who prefer integrated pre-school education in the area.The full and detailed report on which I based  my decision on DP 714 for Bridge IPS is published on my Department’s website and may be accessed at Development Proposal Decisions 2025 | Department of Education

The Department provides dedicated training across the Civil Service on these matters and operates a mediation service to staff who wish to improve working relations quickly and confidentially. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department’s work, alongside the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, to repair the water system in the new maternity hospital.
answer_text: I met recently with interim Chief Executive of the Belfast Trust for an update on their progress in tackling this critical issue.While I remain concerned with issues which have emerged from this project and frustrated with the delays, I have been given assurances by the Trust that they now have a clearer understanding of how they will resolve this issue.A recent interim report has set out the current levels of pseudomonas in the pipework and, vitally, has given some indications to the Trust of the next steps they should take in terms of improving the water quality.The Trust has assured me that they will be working tirelessly to resolve the water safety issues and hope to be in a position by the end of April 2025 to agree a final plan for remedial works and a critical path for the opening of the facility.The department continues to support the Trust and I have instructed that weekly oversight of progress now takes place from one of my senior officials.I know many members share my frustration with the progress on this key project, and I can assure you all that I will continue to focus on this issue until the new maternity hospital is fully operational.

To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to bring rating policy proposals that will support hospitality businesses to the Executive.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the redevelopment of Lurgan Train Station.
To ask the Minister of Finance whether any rates relief support will be provided to childcare facilities in her Rating Policy proposals.
answer_text: 141 registered childcare providers receive rate relief through the Small Business Rate Relief scheme, that support has been extended by my Department for this rating year.The Executive agreed £25 million in the 2024-25 Budget to support the early years and childcare sector. The Childcare Strategy is aimed at making childcare more affordable for parents and families. The analysis provided in relation to supporting the childcare sector through the rating system had highlighted that the support would go towards individual providers with no means to ensure that it would make a significant contribution to the reduction of fees for parents.If the intent of rate relief is to support individual business within this sector, it is a matter for the Education Minister to bring forward a proposal to the Executive as part of his ongoing work in this area.Members should remember every pound provided in new rate relief will be paid for either by other ratepayers in the system paying more or by reduction in public spending. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in addition to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, whether any financial support is being considered to help with the day to day running costs of private nurseries.
answer_text: I secured an additional £7m Executive funding to help stabilise existing early years provision in the current year.  This has enabled me to provide additional payments to all groups delivering Sure Start, Pathway, Toybox, non-statutory Pre-school and other early years programmes, to help them manage rising costs.  It has enabled them to provide pay increases for their staff, while maintaining, and in some cases expanding, front line services.   Calls for further direct government intervention to support early years and childcare providers needs to be considered within the wider economic and business support context.The Department for the Economy is leading on a scoping exercise in conjunction with Invest Northern Ireland, the Department of Health, DE and wider stakeholders, to develop a clearer picture of the business support requirements of the childcare sector and help inform an appropriate government response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to review its policy allowing interchangeability of anti-seizure medication brands, following advice from the Epilepsy Society recommending consistency in brand to ensure stable dosage and to minimise side effects.
answer_text: Different antiepileptic drugs vary considerably in their characteristics, which influences the risk of whether switching between different manufacturers’ products of a particular drug may cause adverse effects or loss of seizure control. Guidance on interchangeability of antiepileptics is produced by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA), following advice received from the Commission of Human Medicines (CHM). This guidance classifies antiepileptics into one of three risk-based categories which help healthcare professionals decide whether it is necessary to maintain continuity of supply of a particular manufacturer’s product. Prescribing recommendations for HSC healthcare professionals, outlined in the Northern Ireland Formulary, incorporate this MHRA guidance. The MHRA guidance indicates that some antiepileptic drugs such as levetiracetam and lacosamide do not need to be prescribed by brand name and can be prescribed generically, as the potential for clinically relevant differences to exist between different manufacturers’ products is considered to be extremely low. It is my Department’s policy that medicines should be prescribed generically in all appropriate circumstances. Generic medicines contain the same drug and are as safe and effective as the branded product, and appropriate use of generic medicines helps us to make better use of limited health service resources because generic medicines are more cost effective.In line with the MHRA advice, clinicians will also consider their patient’s particular circumstances before deciding whether their antiepileptic medicine is suitable for switching to the generic equivalent. Patients will have the opportunity to discuss any proposed change with a healthcare professional before deciding if they wish to try the generic medicine.Where MHRA guidance indicates that a patient should be maintained on a specific manufacturer’s product, this should be prescribed either by specifying a brand name, or by using the generic drug name and name of the manufacturer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to increase the number of electric vehicle charge points in Upper Bann.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of those charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs).Individual CPOs are best placed to advise if they have plans to install further charge points within their wider networks.My officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the timeline for finalising public sector pay awards for 2024-25.
answer_text: While there has been informal engagement with trade unions, the ongoing uncertainty around the budget, means the Civil Service has not yet been able to begin formal negotiations for the 2024 pay award (which was due from 1 August).  I very much hope that there will be sufficient budgetary clarity to enable those negotiations to begin in November.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on maternity services in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Southern Heath and  Social Care Trust have contacted my Department in relation to ongoing workforce challenges across Obstetric and Gynaecology services, particularly at the Daisy Hill site. My officials are currently in daily contact with Southern Trust senior management to develop a comprehensive report which will outline an assessment of the situation; details of the Trust efforts to maintain the current service to date, options under consideration and an appraisal of each option, any external support which would assist the Trust to maintain services at Daisy Hill and the risks associated with this.I want to assure you that my focus is on maintaining accessible and safe in-patient maternity care at Daisy Hill Hospital and my officials are working with the Trust to endeavour to do this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's budgetary position following the in-year budget information gathering exercise.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to introduce a Lynch syndrome registry.
answer_text: Lynch syndrome is an inherited condition that increases your risk of colorectal cancer, endometrial cancer and several other cancers. Doctors estimate that around 3 out of every 100 colon cancers or endometrial cancers are caused by Lynch syndrome. Lynch syndrome also causes cancers to occur at an earlier age than they might in the general population.It is a central tenet of the NI Cancer Strategy to improve early diagnosis. It is therefore important to identify individuals and families with Lynch syndrome so that risk-reducing measures can be taken to address the elevated risk and earlier onset of bowel and other cancers. These include surveillance colonoscopies and gynaecological follow up.  In NI the introduction of testing for Lynch syndrome was successfully implemented in 2019. Our current practice is to test for Lynch syndrome in people with colorectal cancer at ‘high risk’ of the syndrome. This includes people who have a family history of colorectal cancer and those with an early age of onset (younger than 50 years) of colorectal cancer. Testing is also offered to appropriate endometrial cancer patients as part of the Lynch service. I understand that early discussions are underway to assess the feasibility for the establishment of a Northern Ireland Lynch register with input from Queens University Belfast, the NI Cancer registry and HSC staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions he has had with his Executive colleagues regarding the consistently high rates of economic inactivity.
answer_text: Question 9 – AQO 937/22-27Economic inactivity is an important policy area that is led by the Minister for Communities.  I am committed to working with him and other Executive colleagues to help address the barriers to employment.  Our Departments work together to support individuals into employment through the Labour Market Partnerships, where officials from my Skills team and Careers Service are represented. We have also developed joint projects to deliver interventions such as Step Up, JobStart and Entrepreneurship, as well as working together on research and sharing of data to inform future policy development.  DfE officials have been engaging with Community Wealth Building initiatives to explore potential options to reduce economic inactivity in Larne and the North West. There is also collaboration between departments, including Education on the Executive priority to provide high quality, affordable childcare to enable parents and carers to participate in employment and education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to increase the school bus provision for St John the Baptist's College, Portadown, for pupils travelling from the Maghery, Milltown and Columbkille areas.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport which includes route planning and determining the requirements for bus provision.The EA has advised that there is sufficient capacity on the Translink 75 service for St John the Baptist's College pupils travelling from the Maghery, Milltown and Columbkille areas. Whilst it keeps school bus services under review, the EA has no immediate plans to change the service provision in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the budgetary pressures facing her Department.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his engagement with his counterparts in the UK and Ireland on the issue of water quality.
answer_text: I place the highest priority on engagement with both the UK and Ireland on this issue.  Sharing learning, expertise and knowledge is crucial if we are to address the complex water quality issues affecting us all. I have discussed water quality, including the situation at Lough Neagh, and the potential for cooperation, with my Irish Government counterparts at recent North South Ministerial Council Plenary and Environment Sectoral meetings and will continue to raise the issue at future meetings. I had a very useful meeting with Charlie McConalogue TD, Minister for Agriculture, Food and the Marine, last month where we discussed water quality at Lough Neagh.I also had a valuable meeting with Fleur Anderson MP, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Northern Ireland, on 23 August to discuss water quality issues at Lough Neagh where I was able to stress the importance of ongoing financial support from the UK Government and HM Treasury. I was pleased to raise the issue of water quality at the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs which I attended in London on 16 September.  The meeting was hosted by the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs and was attended by Ministers from Defra and the Welsh and Scottish Governments as well by officials from all four UK administrations.  I look forward to attending British-Irish Council Environment Sectoral Meetings and plan to ask that the issue of water quality is added to the agenda for a future meeting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the effectiveness of providing the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy payments in arrears; and (ii) whether there have been delays in providing these payments.
answer_text: The Early Years Organisation (EYO), as the delivery agent for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, has advised that providers can claim the subsidy from them to coincide with their normal billing schedule to ensure they receive it at the same time as the parental contribution. Approximately 75% of providers invoice parents in advance and 25% invoice in arrears.  EYO has also confirmed that there have been no delays in processing payments.  The vast majority have been made within 10 working days of receipt of the claim, except in cases where issues required further investigation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for removing community pharmacies in Banbridge from Sunday and Public Holiday opening hours.
answer_text: During 2023/2024, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group of the Department of Health (SPPG/DoH) in conjunction with the community pharmacy representative body, Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI) completed a review of Community Pharmacy Rota provision across Northern Ireland to ensure that level of provision of community pharmacy services adequately meets the needs of the population. The review process was informed by monitoring of activity during Sunday & Public holiday rotas and feedback provided from participating pharmacies via CPNI.  As part of the review process, feedback on the proposed changes was obtained via a consultation process with the Community/Voluntary and Elected Representatives Engagement Forum.As a result, it was proposed that there was no longer a need for a rota to be provided in Banbridge on Sundays and Public holidays.  No objections to this proposal were received during the consultation period and it was, therefore, agreed to cease rota in Banbridge from 1st July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Bannfoot pedestrian bridge project following the completion of the feasibility study.
answer_text: The proposed Bannfoot Footbridge project is currently at the detailed design stage, with officials in my Department developing the preferred option identified within the feasibility study.  Due to the nature of the location, an Environmental Impact Assessment has been taken forward to determine the effects of such a proposal on the shores of Lough Neagh. This assessment will assist in determining the best technical solution for delivery of the project.Future delivery will be subject to the successful completion of the relevant technical approval processes and availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an updated timeline for the demolition of the derelict Chestnut Place Flats, Banbridge.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The contract for this scheme was originally awarded in Sept 23 however the appointed contractor went into administration. The contract has subsequently been retendered and is in the process of being assessed and expected to start late Autumn of 2024 subject to a successful tender appointment. The initial programme of works is scheduled for a 20-week period with works anticipated to complete by late Spring 2025.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 13043/22-27, whether construction of Moylinn Footbridge, Craigavon, will commence in early 2025.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to taking forward the replacement of Moylinn East Footbridge and I can confirm that planning permission for the replacement bridge was granted on 31st May 2024. It is hoped that construction will commence in early 2025, subject to budget availability and successful contract award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department's budgetary position.
answer_text: My Department’s latest assessment, following implementation of a number of savings, indicates that Education faces inescapable pressures currently totalling circa £190.9m.While I will continue to do everything possible to reduce this figure further, the scale of my Department’s budget shortfall is such that the savings required could only be delivered through an extensive redundancy programme. Similar to the issues faced in other Departments, we simply do not have enough money to meet the increasing demands placed on us.  The Department’s additional resource allocation for 2024-25 is significantly less than what is required and leaves a considerable shortfall across the Education sector.The allocation for education, after June monitoring, means that for the second year in a row, demand-led, statutory pressures will go unfunded. Only the most limited funding is now available for Special Educational Needs, schools, transport, school meals and schools’ maintenance. This will have an extremely damaging impact on the education system, with our children with Special Education Needs facing the most significant impact.  Education has been and remains significantly underfunded.  This is independently evidenced by not only the Institute for Fiscal Studies in its last two comparisons of school spending per pupil across the United Kingdom, but more recently the Independent Review of Education’s report, Investing in a Better Future.Education will require substantially more funding during this budget period, if we are to avoid a significant and detrimental impact on our children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following agreement of the 2024-25 budget, when the Extended Schools Funding will be restored to Ceara School, Lurgan.
answer_text: I was delighted to confirm that the Extended Schools programme will continue to be funded in 2024/25 with a budget of £8.1m. I can confirm that Ceara School is eligible and will receive £15,896 in Extended Schools funding this year.Schools were made aware of their individual allocations on 10 September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7008/22-27, whether the former Lurgan campus of Craigavon Senior High School will be repurposed for an alternative educational use.
answer_text: The Craigavon Senior High School site is currently being internally trawled within all Education Authority (EA) directorates to ascertain if there are any alternative educational or ancillary requirements to retaining the building.If these premises are subsequently declared surplus to requirements, it will go through the agreed public sector disposal process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an up to date estimate of the forecast overspend or underspend for the 2024–25 financial year.
answer_text: At this stage of the financial year, it is too early to predict the year end position.  This will depend on the outcome for further in year exercises and the actions taken by departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the timeline for the 2025–26 Budget.
answer_text: The Chancellor of Exchequer’s statement on 29 July launched a multi-year spending review that will set departmental budgets for the next 3 years. The funding envelope for 2025-26 will be announced on 30 October 2024, with the settlement for further years announced in spring 2025. As a result, the Executive will only be able to set a single year Budget for 2025-26 before the start of the financial year in April.An exercise has already been commissioned to departments to gather information on their requirements for Budget 2025-26 as well as future years. This information is due in advance of the Chancellor’s announcement to ensure the Executive can consider Budget 2025-26 shortly afterwards. It is my intention, subject to Executive agreement, to publish a draft Budget in time to allow for a full twelve week consultation before a final Budget is agreed by the Executive prior to the start of the new financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the 30mph speed limit signage will be erected in Derrymore village, following the introduction of legislation in 2023 for the reduction from the national speed limit.
answer_text: I can advise that the instruction to erect the appropriate 30 mph speed limit signage has been issued, with this work being scheduled for completion after other priority work within the Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon council area.Unfortunately I am not able to confirm an exact timeframe for this work at the present time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his Department can encourage local companies to keep their production lines in Northern Ireland rather than offshoring production.
answer_text: I recognise high operating costs and the lack of skilled labour to aid productivity improvements are some of the challenges facing companies in keeping their production lines in the North of Ireland.I recently launched an Action Plan to grow the Advanced Manufacturing, Materials and Engineering (AMME) sector in the North of Ireland which contains actions that address these challenges and deliver on my key economic priorities. The proposed actions will support investment in skills and labour which have a significant impact on productivity, innovation, and supply chain which often lead to offshore production.  Also, greater investment in R&D and in innovative, cutting-edge technologies and methods to improve efficiency through the City and Growth Deal investments which provide access to automation, smart design, digital twinning, and artificial intelligence.   The Action Plan also supports Invest NI’s range of assistance which can enhance the sustainability and competitiveness of local operations and production lines.  These include interventions designed to maximise local productivity, reduce supply chain risks and costs, leverage opportunities associated with digital transformation and to upskill and reskill existing staff.  Invest NI can also help companies to keep their productions lines in the North of Ireland by providing Grant for R&D support for process improvement to aid efficiency of output and energy savings.  The Department and key delivery partners within the Action Plan will continue to work with companies to ensure they have access to the skills, technologies, R&D, the funding and other supports necessary to reduce costs and increase productivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 5931/22-27, for an update on the cross Executive and local government response following the announcement in February 2024 that Glen Dimplex, Portadown, is to close in 2025.
answer_text: Following the announcement by Glen Dimplex, Invest NI is working closely with the company’s Senior Management team to secure the best outcome for the North of Ireland operation and staff impacted.   The company is also working with both Union Representatives and Salaried Worker Representatives as it enters a consultation periodThe Invest NI team works with the company at both an operational and strategic level. Invest NI is coordinating a cross Executive and local government response to support affected employees at this time. This team will include representatives from both my Department and Invest NI, as well as other stakeholders include Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council, NI Chamber, Trade Unions Manufacturing NI and the local community.  This will ensure that appropriate and timely assistance is available to support workers with redeployment and/or reskilling.  This support is likely to include redundancy clinics, promotion of re-skilling/training opportunities (including Academies), job fairs and identification of job opportunities from across the local industry base.  In addition Invest NI will, through its client engagement, identify and promote job opportunities from the local area to work towards a positive outcome.In addition, my Department’s Careers Service works closely with the Department for the Communities to support anyone facing redundancy or who has been made unemployed. Careers Advisers will be available to provide the employees of Glen Dimplex with personalised professional information, advice and guidance on other local employment, training and education opportunities. Careers Advisers have access to up-to-date labour market information, which they use with clients to advise on how to transfer existing skills and experience to current job opportunities and to inform careers guidance discussions about opportunities for reskilling, training, apprenticeships or in further and higher education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the two year time requirement for entry to stand-alone pharmacist independent prescribing training will be removed.
answer_text: I am aware that following a public consultation, the Council of the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland (the Society) approved proposals in June 2022 to make changes to requirements for training as a pharmacist independent prescriber and being annotated as an independent prescriber on the Register of Pharmaceutical Chemists, which included removing the two-year time requirement for entry onto stand-alone pharmacist independent prescribing training. The Society has set up the Education Reform Implementation Group (ERIG) and is collaborating with the General Pharmaceutical Council (GPhC) and relevant stakeholders to update guidance and address the legislative changes needed for this reform.It is the responsibility of the Society rather than the Department of Health, to make the necessary legislative changes to the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland (General) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1994/202, although my Department has an approval function before the legislation is made.   I understand that the Society is in discussion with its legal advisers on the proposed regulatory changes and to establish a timetable. When clarified, the Society will discuss the proposals and timeline with its partners and submit the draft regulatory changes to my Department for its scrutiny and approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he will deliver his Department's commitments in New Decade, New Approach on workers' rights.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following planning approval, for an indicative timescale of the reinstatement of Moylinn footbridge.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to taking forward the replacement of Moylinn East Footbridge and I can confirm that planning permission for the replacement bridge was granted on 31st May 2024. It is hoped that construction can commence in early 2025 however this is subject to budget availability, successful tender / award and lead in time required by successful bidder for detailed design and fabrication.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their role in driving public service transformation across the Executive.
To ask the Minister of Education whether capital funding will be made available to Portadown Integrated Primary School, subject to planning permission being granted.
answer_text: The project for Portadown Integrated Primary School is in the latter stages of design with the planning application approval anticipated in summer 2024.  The project will proceed to construction when affordable within the wider education budget context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether climate considerations will be included in the proposed Strategic Infrastructure Plan.
answer_text: The interim Fiscal Framework outlines the agreed contents for the Strategic Infrastructure Plan. It states that the Plan will provide further detail on, among other things, the approach to the prioritisation of projects.It will therefore be for the Plan to set out the factors, including climate change, that will be taken into account when prioritising capital spend over the Plan period. The emerging Investment Strategy will inform the development of the Strategic Infrastructure Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will issue a formal response to the Study of Conversion Practices in Northern Ireland, published in May 2024.
answer_text: The previous Minister agreed to award grant funding towards research into conversion practices, which is now complete and I have received the final report.  The findings of the research can form part of an evidence base as the Department progresses to formulate policy around a ban. Further engagement with all those who have an interest in this issue will be required before legislative proposals are prepared. I am considering next steps in policy development and will make an announcement in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how recent allocations from the (i) Levelling Up Fund; and (ii) Shared Prosperity Fund have been distributed.
answer_text: All decisions in relation to the allocation and distribution of Levelling Up Fund (LUF) and the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) have been made by the British Government. Therefore any questions regarding the allocation and distribution of these funds are a matter for the Department of Levelling Up, Housing and Communities.The British Government included £30m which was previously allocated to the North for LUF Round 3, and an unallocated amount of £23m from SPF in the repurposed funding element of the £3.3bn Executive Restoration Package. 	These re-purposed funds totalled £708m, to be allocated equally over 5 years from 2024-25 to 2028-29. This included £235 million ring-fenced for Transformation projects, and the remaining £473 million, or £94.6milion per annum, to be allocated by the Executive. The £94.6 million provided in 2024-25 formed part of the Budget, which was agreed by the Executive in April 2024 and approved by the Assembly in May 2024. The remaining funding will be allocated through future Budget processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact the discontinuation of an October monitoring round will have on Department's budgets.
answer_text: The number, timing and scope of in-year monitoring rounds may vary depending on circumstances in any particular year. Recently the usual approach has been to hold three monitoring rounds per year in June, October and January.  The timing of Westminster fiscal events means that there is more likely to be Barnett consequentials available for allocation in the June and January rounds.  The June round may also allocate any carry forward from the previous year. The October Monitoring round is less likely to have additional funding available from fiscal events. Since 2021-22, departments have more flexibility to manage their budgets without recourse to the Executive, therefore, a reduction to two monitoring rounds will have less impact on internal departmental decisions. In addition, there is usually only a short period between the October monitoring round concluding, and the start of the January Monitoring round, meaning there is limited time for significant budget changes. Therefore, I do not consider that the discontinuation of October monitoring round will have a detrimental impact on departments’ budgets. However, the intention would be to review the need for a third monitoring round in light of specific circumstances each year.For 2024-25 the intention is to hold an October Monitoring round as this will provide an opportunity for the Executive to consider the results of the departmental equality screening/ impact assessments on decisions relating to Budget 2024-25 and determine if any budget adjustments are required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, further to the Interim Fiscal Framework, how her Department intends to provide independent evidence to Treasury in relation to the needs-based factor.
To ask the Minister of Health, in order to assist with waiting list reductions in Northern Ireland, whether he plans to reopen the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: Following the success of the scheme I introduced in June 2021, I had hoped to introduce a new reimbursement scheme this year. I included a bid for funding for a new replacement, which could have treated over 4,000 patients, during this year’s budget setting process. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will provide financial support for the creation and maintenance of athletics facilities.
answer_text: Article 10 of The Recreation and Youth Service (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 records the responsibility of provision for facilities for recreation, social, physical and cultural activities with the district council, therefore, this is initially a matter for the relevant Local Authorities.At this time there are no current programmes within my Department to which an application could be made for the creation and maintenance of athletics facilities. This is also an operational issue for Sport NI and I have sought information from the Chief Executive regarding financial support for athletics and he has advised:“Sport NI, through its Multi Facility Fund programme, is currently working with Mid Ulster District Council to develop an eight-lane athletics track and 3G synthetic pitch infill at the Mid Ulster Sports Arena.  MUDC has submitted a planning application and a planning decision is expected this summer.  Thereafter work will continue to procure an Integrated Supply Team. Sport NI is also working on a Sports Infrastructure Review and will be using this to better understand the existing facilities and needs for the sector. Engagement with sports (including Athletics NI) will be part of this review which will shape future investment plans.Sport NI have provided the following investment over the previous five financial periods: £100,000 in Athletics facilities£92,868.00 in support of Athletics clubs and projects£1,581,354.00 for Athletics NI £480,000 awarded to Athletics NI for FY24-25”Due to the volume of the information, I have arranged for a breakdown of the awards provided by Sport NI to be placed in the Assembly Library.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with Sport NI will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following his Ministerial Statement on 30 April 2024, to detail any plans being progressed for increasing provision for Special Schools in Upper Bann.
answer_text: There are two existing special schools in Upper Bann - Donard School, Banbridge and Ceara School, Lurgan. Following my Statement on 30 April 2024, the Department of Education is working with the Education Authority to undertake a review of all special schools in Northern Ireland to determine where additional places are needed and how the additional capacity could be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the difficulty that patients at Donaghcloney Surgery, Co. Down have had in accessing GP appointments; and (ii) whether any action can be taken to alleviate this pressure.
answer_text: The GP Federation was appointed the new contractor of Donaghcloney Surgery with effect from 01 January 2024.   I recognise that being able to access GP services when we need to do so is important for all of us.  GP practices are under significant pressures at present as they deal with immediate demands and the consequences of wider service pressures.  As independent contractors, GPs practices manage the day to day running of their practices and must ensure that any decisions made are clinically appropriate for the patient.  Should patients have concerns regarding the services provided by Donaghcloney Surgery, this should be raised with the GP practice itself in the first instance.  All practices are required to have a complaint procedure to address any issues raised by patients. Alternatively, the SPPG Complaints Team can act as an intermediary between a patient and the practice.  The SPPG Complaints Team can be contacted for further advice and assistance by e-mail: complaints.sppg@hscni.net, by telephone 028 95363893, by text phone 18001 028 95363893, by calling in person or by writing to the SPPG Complaints Team, 12-22 Linenhall Street, Belfast BT2 8BS.  As part of the 3yearly GMS Practice Visits, Donaghcloney Surgery are due a practice visit at which SPPG colleagues will discuss access with the contract holders and will continue to keep the situation under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the Royal College of Pathologists Risk Assessment Report into the Cervical Cytology Service provided by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, whether any cytology staff were reported to the Health and Care Professions Council or other relevant professional bodies.
answer_text: At this time, no screeners have been referred to the Heath and Care Professions Council (HCPC) by the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an indicative timescale for the funding allocation to Lurgan Model Primary School following the recent planning approval for an extension and stand-alone nursery unit.
answer_text: I welcome the increased capital budget allocation for the Department of Education for 2024-25. However, given the quantum of need in regard to Special Educational Needs, it will not meet all of my Department’s inescapable capital requirements and difficult decisions around prioritisation will be taken over the coming weeks. As part of this process, Lurgan Model Primary School will be considered alongside all of my Department’s priorities. I will continue to make the case for increased investment in the education estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department plans to introduce socialisation of grid connection charges.
To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the need for the Executive to establish multi-year budgets in the future.
answer_text: I have made clear to Executive colleagues, my desire to set a multi-year budget to allow departments to plan more effectively, however this is dependent on the period covered by the next Spending Review.2024-25 is the last year of the current Spending Review period, and therefore the Executive can only develop a one year budget. I have pressed both the Chief Secretary to the Treasury and the shadow Secretary of State on the need for a multi-year settlement going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will answer AQW 6461/22-27.
answer_text: The response for AQW 6461/22-27 issued on 17 April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 5923/22-27, what plans he has in place to expand Donard School, Banbridge.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) recently acquired additional land adjacent to Donard School, which will enable the current school site to be extended.  This will facilitate the provision of additional accommodation to meet the increased demand for places in the school for the medium- and longer-term needs of pupils requiring special school education in the Banbridge area.  The EA is currently working with the school to deliver short-term solutions to address the need for September 2024 in the Banbridge area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on capital works planned for (i) Brownlow Health Centre; and (ii) Gilford Health Centre.
answer_text: Contractors have been appointed to provide modular units to create additional accommodation for the GP surgeries located in Brownlow Health Centre and Gilford Health Centre.  It is anticipated that the space will be available by the end of the calendar year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress made to complete the capital works at Portadown Health Centre.
answer_text: The capital works at Portadown Health Centre are currently in the latter stages with construction works due to complete at the end of April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of a fuel poverty strategy.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 6599/22–27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to modify Rural Community Transport Partnerships' boundaries to bring them in line with the amalgamated council boundaries.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking a phased review of the community transport schemes which it funds, including the Dial-A-Lift Scheme provided by the Rural Community Transport Partnerships. The issue of the boundaries in which the Rural Community Transport Partnerships operate was raised during the engagement undertaken with the Partnerships and service users under the Phase 1 Evaluation stage of the review. The issues raised in Phase 1 will be considered when an action plan for Phase 2 of the review is developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how she intends to incorporate climate considerations into the budget process.
answer_text: Under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, all departments are required to exercise their functions in a manner that is consistent with achieving emissions targets and carbon budgets as far as possible.As climate action is so far reaching, it should be a consideration right across spending areas in departments and not limited to actions that can be funded from additional allocations in what is a very constrained financial environment.Therefore, I will be encouraging my Executive colleagues to consider the statutory obligations in the Climate Change Act when prioritising expenditure within the funding envelopes provided to their departments in the Budget 2024-25.Of course, any requests for additional funding from departments for specific actions to address climate change will be considered by the Executive alongside other priorities in the context of the funding available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to provide further transitional support for young carers in schools following the launch of the Young Carers Covenant in March 2024.
answer_text: My department continues to work with stakeholders to improve the understanding in schools around the role of young carers, the challenges they face and the support available.  I, therefore, welcome the publication of the Young Carers Covenant.  In January 2024, my department issued a ‘Young Carers in Education Digital Awareness Pack’ to all schools. The pack was developed with Action for Children, Barnardo’s Northern Ireland and the Education Authority’s Education Welfare Service. The pack includes a digital poster designed by a young carer, guidance on ‘Supporting Young Carers in School’ and a research report produced by Action for Children’s Young Carers Reference Group. I also recently nominated officials to sit on the newly established Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group for Carers.   My officials will advocate for the needs of young carers in this forum as well as working collaboratively with other departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the Portadown Flood Alleviation Scheme.
answer_text: The Portadown Flood Alleviation scheme is currently at the detailed design stage and work is ongoing to complete the design and finalise the business case. 	This work includes refining aspects of the proposed defences following completion of tree surveys and consultation on an addendum to the Environmental Statement for the scheme. 	Consultation on the Environmental Statement Addendum will commence in April.Subject to gaining the necessary approvals and the availability of funding, it is currently estimated that the procurement process to appoint a contractor for the works could commence in Autumn 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 7946/22-27, whether she will include assessing the cost of division in the statutory remit of the Fiscal Council.
answer_text: It is not my intention to include a specific requirement to assess the cost of division within the statutory remit of the Fiscal Council.  Rather I will be seeking the Executive’s agreement to introduce legislation that broadly aligns with the Council’s current Terms of Reference, which is to assess and report on the current and future state of our public finances. In line with the OECD principles for Independent Fiscal Institutions, and similarly to other such organisations, it will then be for the Council to determine its own work programme within the bounds of this broad mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when the findings from the Review of Defamation Law consultation will be published.
answer_text: Section 11 of the Defamation Act (NI) 2022 requires that the Department of Finance review defamation law in this jurisdiction and relevant developments elsewhere, and report its findings and recommendations to the Assembly by June 2024. To inform its review, the Department engaged with key stakeholders in 2023-24. The results of this exercise will be reflected in the June 2024 report to the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the redevelopment of Banbridge Public Library.
answer_text: Supported by the Department, Libraries NI acquired a potential development site in 2021/22 for a new library facility in Banbridge.  Libraries NI has advised that the site acquisition is one step in a process and that a business case supporting the proposed redevelopment will be drafted in due course and submitted for consideration by the Department.  Libraries NI has no definitive timescale for the development of the business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to progress the link road from Armagh Road to Tandragee Road, Portadown, via Mahon Road.
answer_text: The Mahon Road Link is identified in the Craigavon Area Plan 2010 as a development related scheme.  As such, my Department has no plans to progress or implement this road scheme. However, my Department will, through the planning process, continue to protect the privately owned road line from development, which would potentially prejudice the implementation of the scheme in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will commission an independent review of domestic and non-domestic rates relief.
To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to the recommendations of the Independent Review of Education, whether he intends to reform academic selection.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering all of the findings of the Independent Review of Education.An internal consultation process is currently underway.   It is anticipated that this process will culminate in a paper for my consideration by the end of April 2024. I will then be in a position to consider options for implementation of all of the report’s recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department will work with NI Water to take remedial action to ensure all outstanding works in Hunters Lodge, Lurgan, are completed.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will commit to providing funding for active travel schemes in Upper Bann.
answer_text: Active Travel schemes in Upper Bann that are currently programmed for delivery in the 24/25 financial year are Mandeville Road, linking Drumgor and Lisnisky, Oak Path linking the proposed Moylinn footbridge to Tullygally and the Cherry path which links the underpass at South Lakes Leisure centre to the Drumgor Centre.  The replacement Active Travel bridge at Moylinn is also being programmed to start on-site in the 24/25 financial year and I have already given a commitment that the bridge at Bannfoot will progress to detailed design stage.Future delivery will be subject to the successful completion of the relevant processes and availability of funding. I am enthusiastic about the positive change that active travel can make to the lives of people who want to have greater choice in how they travel, particularly for shorter everyday journeys, or in combination with using public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the operation of the Abortion Services (Safe Access Zones) Act (Northern Ireland) 2023 at Craigavon Area Hospital.
answer_text: Safe Access Zones were introduced at all health service locations providing abortion services across Northern Ireland from 29 September 2023, and continue to operate effectively in line with Assembly legislation to protect women and girls accessing abortion treatment services, information, advice and counselling, and to protect staff working at these locations. My Department's website includes a list and maps of Safe Access Zones, FAQs and Operator Guidance.In line with the requirements under Section 8 of the Abortion Services (Safe Access Zones) Act (Northern Ireland) 2023, the Department will publish an annual report setting out whether, in the opinion of the Department, each safe access zone has been effective in protecting the safety and dignity of protected persons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when reforms to marriage law in Northern Ireland will be implemented following the 2022 consultation.
answer_text: I am currently considering policy on this important issue with a view to securing inclusion of a Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill in the Executive’s legislative programme. This will aim to put belief marriage on an equal legislative footing with civil and religious marriage and increase the minimum age for marriage and civil partnership from 16 to 18, both of which were strongly supported during consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department's plans to give approval to NIE Networks to introduce the roll-out of smart meters following the pilot programme.
answer_text: The Department completed a cost benefit analysis of smart meters (CBA) in early 2023. The CBA report concluded that the case for the implementation of electricity smart meters had produced a significant level of benefit for consumers. The Department has commenced planning the implementation of electricity smart meters. It is currently exploring the roles and responsibilities for industry, a provider has not yet been selected. Following the completion of a Design Plan report the Department will consult on the design for a roll-out later this year. The introduction of a new system requires legislation to instruct a deployment plus associated licence conditions for relevant companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the increase in the rate of Air Passenger Duty announced by the UK government, to detail the resulting cost to the Executive's budget.
answer_text: The changes announced at Spring Budget 2024 to Air Passenger Duty (APD) will not have a direct impact on the adjustment that is made to the Executive’s budget in relation to the devolution of direct long haul APD rate setting powers.This adjustment is linked to the overall growth in long-haul passenger numbers departing airports across Britain and the North, not rates or revenues.The reduction to the Block Grant for 2024-25 remains £2.445m. Future adjustments will be determined at the next Spending Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the implementation of the recommendations from the RHI Inquiry Report.
answer_text: Significant work has been done on reforms recommended by the Renewable Heat Incentive Inquiry. The Department of Finance has published an update of the implementation of the recommendations of the RHI Inquiry on the departmental website which can be viewed here.Action has been taken and tangible outputs can be demonstrated. The lessons from the Inquiry, along with the recommendations of the Public Accounts Committee and other external reviews into the Civil Service, have formed the basis for a longer-term body of work to ensure the service is able to fulfil its functions to the highest standard. A sub-group of the NICS board has been established to put in place a one-year (2024/2025) people plan and to lead the development of a 2025-2030 People and Transformation Strategy. This five-year strategy will align with the Programme for Government and support the people, organisational, leadership and change priorities for the future in a focused and coordinated way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she intends to place the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council on a statutory footing.
answer_text: I will be seeking agreement from the Executive in the coming weeks on a draft policy document to instruct the Office of the Legislative Counsel to begin the formal drafting process for a Fiscal Council Bill that will place the Council on a statutory footing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's plans to address the backlog of MOT test appointments.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has adopted a range of measures to increase its vehicle testing capacity, including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners and offering vehicle testing appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition launched on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.In 2022-2023, the DVA conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.In addition, vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at Change, cancel or view your MOT/ vehicle test appointment online | nidirect.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps will be implemented to ensure GP coverage for Portadown, following the planned closures of the Waterside and Annagh practices.
answer_text: On 23 February 2024, Annagh Family Practice and on 27 February 2024, Waterside Medical Practice notified the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH) that they were withdrawing from their contract to deliver General Medical Services.  Following completion of the notice period, their contract will end on 31 August 2024.   The SPPG will begin the process to secure alternative arrangements for these services.  The preferred option is to secure a GP contractor or grouping of GPs to take over the practice through a formal recruitment process.  The Annagh Family Practice and Waterside Medical Practice will continue to deliver GP services and patients do not need to take any action.  They should continue to contact their practice as normal.  SPPG has commenced the process of informing the patients of both practices.  To ensure stability for all GP practices in the area, the SPPG has temporarily suspended the ability of the patients of Annagh Family Practice and Waterside Medical Practice to change GP practices.  This is only a temporary move until a new contractor is appointed and services are stabilised.  Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts, with the support of GP Federations and Trusts in taking on contracts, the Department has been able to ensure that no practice has closed and that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will introduce legislation to ban no-fault evictions.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the sustainability of the newly-appointed GP contract in Donaghcloney, Co. Down.
answer_text: The GP Federation were appointed as the contract holder for the Donaghcloney Practice with effect from 01 January 2024.The GP Federation has set up a Community Interest Company specifically for the running of Donaghacloney Practice. The GP Federation as the contractor for the practice is very well placed to ensure the continued delivery of GMS services to the patients registered with the practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will introduce legislation to set up a renewable energy support scheme.
answer_text: My department is currently developing a support scheme for renewable electricity which we intend to introduce as soon as possible.My officials are currently working on a scheme design to promote investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects, guarantee fair pricing for locally produced electricity, and encourage a diverse range of renewable sources for a secure energy supply. The Primary and Subordinate legislation required is being carefully considered and will be introduced in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the status of funding from the UK Shared Prosperity Fund.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund continues to be delivered here. A portion of the Fund, which was previously subject to the British Government’s pause and review has been made available to the Executive as part of the Financial Package.  Any decisions in relation to this will be a matter for the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when repairs will be carried out to Banbridge Youth Resource Centre to allow the centre to fully reopen.
answer_text: Banbridge Youth Resource Centre has experienced several serious plumbing issues. The most recent was caused by a burst pipe, resulting in secondary damage to the facility, which now requires significant maintenance. The Education Authority (EA) is working towards resolving these issues but are unable to provide a re-opening date at this time.  In the meantime, EA is actively seeking alternative venues to ensure the young people of the area have access to similar activities and projects during this temporary closure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional support his Department can provide to young carers.
answer_text: In supporting young carers, my Department commissions the Northern Ireland Regional Young Carers Service, which is a regional service offering young carers individual support, short breaks, advocacy, advice and signposting. This support is delivered across the region by two voluntary sector organisations, Action for Children and Barnardo’s NI. Young people are eligible for the service if they are aged between 8-18, have a substantial caring role for a member of their family, and whose caring responsibilities have an impact on one or more of the following: their education, physical health, emotional wellbeing, and provides opportunities for social interaction with their peers. The service also includes the provision of information, group work and one to one support. My Department will continue with its ongoing efforts to support our young carers, and we are committed to providing those supports both practical and financial for them, resources permitting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will provide the capital funding to facilitate the new build of Portadown Integrated Primary School, subject to a successful planning application.
answer_text: The project for Portadown Integrated Primary School is in the latter stages of design with the planning application having been submitted in late 2023.  The project will proceed to construction when affordable within the wider education budget context.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's plans to address the backlog of 700 outstanding care packages in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is a regional challenge with the recruitment and retention of domiciliary care staff across Statutory and Independent Sector Providers.  The demand for community care packages currently outstrips the supply of available staff to take on this work.  The Southern Health and Social Care Trust is currently providing a greater number of Domiciliary Care hours per year than it is funded to do so and there still remains substantial unmet need for packages of care.  There has been an additional investment of £5M allocated to the HSC Trusts for 2023/2024 (recurrently) to specifically target independent sector provision of care packages for the over 65 population. Following the publication of an Evaluation and Summary Report on the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation, in May 2023, the Department established the Social Care Collaborative Forum (SCCF) to commence the delivery of reform, with a number of workstreams established across a range of key issues. SCCF workstreams are developing a plan to grow capacity in domiciliary care across the region, develop a new model for domiciliary care and take forward key aspects of social care workforce development.In addition, HSC Trusts have been allocated recurrent funding of £697,000 to establish Early Review Teams. The Early Review Teams are being piloted currently across all five HSC Trusts and are designed to bring some of the previously allocated domiciliary care hours back into the system by ensuring reassessment of need within two to eight weeks of hospital discharge. Any hours released from the pilot will provide further capacity into the domiciliary care service.Domiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures; however, my Department remains committed to the provision of a high-quality domiciliary care service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the implementation of the Energy Strategy Action Plan 2023.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy Action Plan 2023 was published on 15th March 2023 Energy Strategy Action Plan 2023 | Department for the Economy (economy-ni.gov.uk) and a report to update on progress against the Actions was published on 1st February 2024 and can be found at the following link: Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2023 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an updated timeline for the large-scale improvement works in the vicinity of Bogglehill Road, Lawrencetown, Co. Down.
answer_text: Bogglehill Road will be considered for inclusion in the Department’s future resurfacing programme subject to budget availability and its relative priority when compared to the many other roads in need of investment. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council which will be published once budgets are finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to update the statutory remit of the Utility Regulator to include (i) decarbonisation; and (ii) climate change targets in their duties.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator has an integral role to play in supporting my Department on the decarbonisation journey, and in delivering the objectives of both the Executive’s Energy Strategy ‘The Path to Net Zero’ and the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022.  Officials are working closely with UR colleagues and Departmental Solicitor’s Office to understand the organisation’s duties and explore whether a legislative amendment is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her Department's plans for the future use of Marlborough House, Craigavon.
answer_text: Marlborough House is significantly beyond the capacity needed by the Civil Service in Craigavon and is among our worst performing buildings in terms of energy efficiency.The Department of Finance therefore intends to vacate the property in the next 18 months and commence the appropriate disposal process. My officials are currently exploring options to source suitable, efficient and modern accommodation for civil servants working in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of the Southern Health and Social Care Trust's Cervical Screening Review.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what resurfacing schemes will be taken forward in Upper Bann as part of the recent investment announcement.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. As such, I have taken action to begin to address this issue by announcing the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs and deteriorating road conditions.  This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective along with some larger scale resurfacing projects.  However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure. The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council area was presented to Council members in October 2023 and can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-council-report-armagh-banbridge-craigavon-2023.pdf .The resurfacing programme along with other programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether consideration will be given to (i) a graduated reduction in speed on Castlewellan Road, Banbridge, close to Ballydown Primary School; and (ii) the installation of a crash barrier outside Ballydown Primary School.
answer_text: The Department’s policy for setting the appropriate speed limit for a road aims to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.
